title: 3 Challenges and 2 Hopes for the Safety of Unsupervised Elicitation
abstract: We study 3 realistic challenges to the safety of unsupervised elicitation and easy-to-hard generalization techniques, which aim to steer models on tasks which are beyond human supervision. We create datasets to test the robustness of methods against these challenges. We stress-test existing techniques on them along with new methods relying on 2 hopes: ensembling and combining unsupervised and easy-to-hard methods. We find that although the new hopes sometimes perform better than other approaches, no technique reliably performs well on the 3 challenges.

title: GPT-5.4 Thinking System Card

title: Reasoning models struggle to control their chains of thought, and that’s good
abstract: OpenAI introduces CoT-Control and finds reasoning models struggle to control their chains of thought, reinforcing monitorability as an AI safety safeguard.

title: Evidence for inference scaling in AI cyber tasks: Increased evaluation budgets reveal higher success rates
abstract: Alongside Irregular, we found evidence demonstrating that evaluators need to use large token budgets to understand the cyber capabilities of recent Large Language Models (LLMs).

title: Phi-4-reasoning-vision and the lessons of training a multimodal reasoning model
authors: Jyoti Aneja, Michael Harrison, Neel Joshi, Tyler LaBonte, John Langford, Eduardo Salinas
abstract: We are pleased to announce Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B, a 15 billion parameter open‑weight multimodal reasoning model, available through Microsoft Foundry (opens in new tab), HuggingFace (opens in new tab) and GitHub (opens in new tab). Phi-4-reasoning-vision-15B is a broadly capable model that can be used for a wide array of vision-language tasks such as image captioning, asking […] The post Phi-4-reasoning-vision and the lessons of training a multimodal reasoning model appeared first on Microsoft Research .

title: Extending single-minus amplitudes to gravitons
abstract: A new preprint extends single-minus amplitudes to gravitons, with GPT-5.2 Pro helping derive and verify nonzero graviton tree amplitudes in quantum gravity.

title: The People's Liberation Army's Perspectives on Artificial Intelligence Highlighting Integration as Key to "Intelligentization" Goals
abstract: Expert Insights Mar 4, 2026 The authors reviewed academic journals and news media to determine the People's Liberation Army's views on integrating artificial intelligence into its military operations as part of China's larger "intelligentization" strategy.

title: An Alignment Journal: Coming Soon
abstract: tl;dr We’re incubating an academic journal for AI alignment: rapid peer-review of under-served foundational Alignment research.

abstract: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is our fastest and most cost-efficient Gemini 3 series model yet.

title: How to Design Environments for Understanding Model Motives
abstract: Authors: Gerson Kroiz*, Aditya Singh*, Senthooran Rajamanoharan, Neel Nanda Gerson and Aditya are co-first authors. This work was conducted during MATS 9.0 and was advised by Senthooran Rajamanoharan and Neel Nanda. TL;DR Understanding why a model took an action is a key question in AI Safety. It is a difficult question, as often many motivations could plausibly lead to the same action. We are particularly concerned about the case of model incrimination, i.e. observing a model taking a bad action and distinguishing between benign and malign motivations, e.g. scheming vs confusion. To practice the skill of understanding motivations, we need to build high-quality environments that serve as realistic proxy tasks for investigating future models. But we’ve found that there are many ways in which environments can be contrived. It is valuable to invest effort in ruling out confounds like ambiguity, instruction conflict, or confusion.
